questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'StaticSummary' field you can provide when starting a Workflow in Temporal?

choices.text: It sets a password that protects the Workflow from unauthorized access in the UI.
It provides a short, one-line description that appears in the Workflow list view to help identify the Workflow.
It stores the Workflow's input data in a compressed format for faster retrieval.
It defines the maximum number of times the Workflow can be retried if it fails.

questions.explanation: StaticSummary is a brief, single-line label (up to 200 bytes) set at Workflow start time that shows up in the Workflow list view in the Temporal UI. This makes it easy to identify what a Workflow is doing at a glance without clicking into it. Think of it like a subject line for your Workflow — it stays fixed for the life of the Workflow, unlike 'CurrentDetails' which can be updated as the Workflow progresses.

questions.question: A Temporal Worker is running and waiting for tasks. What does it actually do when work becomes available?

choices.text: It sends an HTTP request to the Temporal Service asking for a new Workflow to be created from scratch.
It pushes completed task results directly to the client application that started the Workflow.
It polls a Task Queue for tasks, dequeues them, and then executes the corresponding Workflow or Activity logic.
It monitors the Temporal Web UI for new Workflow submissions and triggers execution from there.

questions.explanation: Workers continuously poll a Task Queue on the Temporal Service, looking for tasks to execute. When a Workflow or Activity task is available, the Worker dequeues it and runs the corresponding code. This polling model means Workers don't need to be directly reachable — they reach out to Temporal, which makes it easy to run Workers behind firewalls or in private networks. Without Workers, your Workflow and Activity code would never actually run.

questions.question: In Temporal, what is an Activity designed to handle compared to a Workflow?

choices.text: Activities manage the overall business logic and long-running orchestration, while Workflows handle one-time, stateless operations.
Activities perform single, well-defined actions that often interact with the outside world, such as calling an API or writing to a database, while Workflows orchestrate those actions.
Activities store durable state across restarts, while Workflows are stateless and restart from the beginning on each execution.
Activities run directly on the Temporal Service, while Workflows run only inside the client application that starts them.

questions.explanation: Activities are where you put code that touches the outside world — sending emails, making HTTP calls, reading from a database — things that can fail for reasons outside your control. Workflows, on the other hand, orchestrate those Activities and contain your business logic. If an Activity fails, Temporal automatically retries it based on your retry policy. Keeping side effects in Activities and orchestration in Workflows is the key pattern that makes Temporal applications reliable and predictable.

questions.question: In the Temporal .NET SDK, what is the key behavioral difference between `StaticSummary`/`StaticDetails` set in `WorkflowOptions` and `Workflow.CurrentDetails` set inside the Workflow?

choices.text: `StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are only visible in the Event History, while `Workflow.CurrentDetails` appears exclusively in the Workflow list view alongside other running Workflows.

`StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are fixed at Workflow start and cannot change, while `Workflow.CurrentDetails` can be updated repeatedly throughout the Workflow's execution to reflect current status.
`StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` support full HTML formatting, while `Workflow.CurrentDetails` is limited to plain text with no Markdown support.
`StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are set by the Worker process, while `Workflow.CurrentDetails` must be set by the client that started the Workflow.

questions.explanation: `StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are provided in `WorkflowOptions` at the time the Workflow is started and remain fixed for the Workflow's lifetime — they can't be changed once set. `Workflow.CurrentDetails`, assigned inside the `[WorkflowRun]` method, can be updated at any point during execution, making it ideal for reflecting dynamic status like 'processing step 3 of 10'. All three fields support Markdown (excluding images, HTML, and scripts), so option C is wrong. Both are set from their respective sides of the Temporal boundary (client vs. workflow code), but the distinction in option D about the Worker is incorrect — `StaticSummary`/`StaticDetails` come from the client, not the Worker. Option A is wrong about which views these fields appear in.

questions.question: You want to attach a short contextual label to an Activity execution so it appears inline next to the Activity event in the Temporal Web UI Event History without having to expand the event. Which API do you use in the .NET SDK?

choices.text: Set `StaticDetails` on `WorkflowOptions` when starting the parent Workflow, which propagates to all child Activity events automatically.
Set `Workflow.CurrentDetails` inside the Workflow before calling `ExecuteActivityAsync` so the label is captured in the surrounding context.
Return a descriptive string from the Activity method itself; Temporal automatically uses the return value as the event summary in the UI.
Set the `Summary` property on `ActivityOptions` when calling `Workflow.ExecuteActivityAsync`, which attaches the label directly to that Activity's event.

questions.explanation: The `ActivityOptions` class in the .NET SDK has a `Summary` property (limited to 200 bytes) that attaches a label directly to the Activity's scheduled event. This summary appears in purple text in the Event History next to the Activity event, providing immediate context at a glance. `StaticDetails` on `WorkflowOptions` (option A) describes the Workflow itself and does not propagate to Activities. `Workflow.CurrentDetails` (option B) updates the dynamic Workflow-level detail panel, not individual Activity events. The Activity's return value (option C) is captured as output data, not as a UI summary. You'd reach for `ActivityOptions.Summary` any time you want operators or developers to quickly understand what a specific Activity invocation is doing without expanding the event.

questions.question: When setting up a Temporal .NET Worker in the Hello World example, which statement correctly describes how Activities and Workflows are registered on the `TemporalWorker`?

choices.text: Workflows are registered by passing a generic type parameter (`AddWorkflow<T>()`), while instance Activities are registered by passing a bound method reference (e.g., `activities.SayHello`) to `AddActivity()`.
Both Workflows and Activities are registered by passing an instance of the class to a single `Register()` method; the SDK reflects on the class to find attributed members automatically.
Workflows are registered by passing a constructed instance, while Activities are registered by passing the class type using a generic parameter such as `AddActivity<MyActivities>()`.
Both Workflows and Activities are registered using generic type parameters; the difference is that Activity classes must be marked `static` while Workflow classes must be `sealed`.

questions.explanation: In the .NET SDK, `TemporalWorkerOptions` distinguishes between how Workflows and Activities are registered. Workflows use the generic `AddWorkflow<SayHelloWorkflow>()` method — Temporal manages instantiation. Activities, however, are registered as method references on an already-constructed instance (e.g., `activities.SayHello`), because the Activity class may hold state like database connections or configuration. Option B describes a single reflection-based API that doesn't exist. Option C reverses the pattern — Activities take instances, not type parameters. Option D is incorrect; Activities don't need to be static and Workflows don't need to be sealed, though static Activities are also supported as an alternative style.

questions.question: What is the size limit for the `StaticSummary` field set in `WorkflowOptions`, and what Markdown restrictions apply to all summary and details fields in Temporal's .NET SDK?

choices.text: `StaticSummary` is limited to 500 bytes and supports all CommonMark Markdown including embedded images provided they use HTTPS URLs.
`StaticSummary` is limited to 200 bytes, and all summary/details fields support Markdown but exclude images, HTML, and scripts.
`StaticSummary` is limited to 20K bytes to match `StaticDetails`, and both fields only accept plain text with no Markdown rendering.
`StaticSummary` has no enforced byte limit but is displayed truncated at 200 characters in the UI; Markdown is fully supported including inline HTML.

questions.explanation: `StaticSummary` (and the `Summary` field on Activities/timers) is capped at 200 bytes and is intended as a single-line description. `StaticDetails` has a much larger limit of 20K bytes for richer multi-line content. All fields accept standard Markdown but explicitly exclude images, HTML, and scripts — this is a consistent security/rendering boundary across all metadata fields. Option A is wrong about both the size limit and the image allowance. Option C confuses the limits for `StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` and incorrectly states no Markdown is supported. Option D mischaracterizes the limit as a display truncation rather than an enforced byte cap and incorrectly allows inline HTML.

questions.question: Your team deploys a new version of a long-running .NET Workflow that updates `Workflow.CurrentDetails` at several checkpoints during execution. After deploy, you notice in the Temporal Web UI that older in-flight Workflow executions still show the static summary set at start, while newly started executions show live status updates mid-run. A junior engineer asks why the older runs don't reflect the new dynamic details logic. What is the most accurate explanation?

choices.text: `Workflow.CurrentDetails` is a write-once field; once a Workflow execution starts, any code that attempts to update it is silently ignored until the next Workflow continuation via Continue-As-New.

The older in-flight executions are replaying from their event history. Because `Workflow.CurrentDetails` assignments haven't been reached yet in the replay, the UI shows the last persisted value, which is the static summary until the Workflow advances past those new checkpoints.

Static summary set at Workflow start permanently overrides any dynamic details for that execution; `Workflow.CurrentDetails` can only take effect on Workflows started after the field was first written.
The Temporal Web UI caches Workflow metadata for up to 5 minutes; the older executions will reflect the updated `CurrentDetails` once the cache expires and the server re-reads the latest event.

questions.explanation: In Temporal's .NET SDK, `Workflow.CurrentDetails` is a dynamic value that can be updated throughout Workflow execution — but it only reflects the latest value that has been *committed* to the event history during actual forward progress. For older in-flight executions that haven't yet reached the new code path (because they are waiting on an Activity, timer, or signal), the UI will show whatever value was last persisted. There is no permanent override from static summary, no 5-minute cache, and no write-once restriction. The static summary/details set at `WorkflowOptions` start time are separate fields from `CurrentDetails` and don't suppress it.

questions.question: A payment processing Workflow in .NET executes five sequential Activities. An engineer adds a `Summary` of `"Charging customer credit card"` to the charge Activity's `ActivityOptions` and a `Summary` of `"Waiting for bank settlement"` to a subsequent `Workflow.DelayWithOptionsAsync` timer. After running in production, the team reviews the Temporal Web UI. Where exactly will these summaries appear, and what is the byte limit each must respect?

choices.text: Both summaries appear only in the Workflow Overview section alongside static summary and details. Each summary is limited to 20K bytes, matching the `StaticDetails` limit.
Both summaries appear in the Event History next to their corresponding events in purple text, and are also visible when an event is expanded. Each summary is limited to 200 bytes.

Activity summaries appear in the Event History, but timer summaries appear only in the Workflow Overview section. Both are limited to 200 bytes, but timer summaries also support full Markdown including images.

Both summaries appear exclusively inside the expanded event detail view and are not visible in the collapsed Event History list. The limit for both is 2KB to accommodate multi-line Markdown descriptions.

questions.explanation: According to the documentation, Activity and Timer summaries both appear in the Event History — in purple text next to their corresponding events when collapsed, and also prominently in the expanded event detail view. Neither appears in the Workflow Overview section (that section is for Workflow-level static summary, static details, and current details). The byte limit for `Summary` on both Activities and timers is 200 bytes. The 20K byte limit applies only to `StaticDetails` set at Workflow start, not to event-level summaries. Timer summaries follow the same Markdown restrictions as other metadata fields (no images, HTML, or scripts).

questions.question: Your operations team wants to display rich operational context in the Temporal Web UI Workflow list view — specifically, a one-sentence description identifying which customer and order type each Workflow instance represents. They also want a multi-paragraph status report visible when drilling into a specific Workflow. Which combination of `WorkflowOptions` fields satisfies both requirements, and what are their respective size constraints?

choices.text: Use `StaticSummary` (200-byte limit) for the one-sentence description in the list view, and `StaticDetails` (20K-byte limit) for the multi-paragraph report in the details view.
Use `StaticSummary` (20K-byte limit) for the list view and `Workflow.CurrentDetails` (200-byte limit) for the details view; `StaticDetails` is reserved for system-generated metadata only.
Use `StaticDetails` (200-byte limit) for both views; the UI automatically truncates the value for the list view and shows the full value in the details view.
Use `Workflow.CurrentDetails` for the list view (200-byte limit) and `StaticSummary` for the details view (20K-byte limit); the field names are misleading relative to where they appear in the UI.

questions.explanation: `StaticSummary` is explicitly designed for the Workflow list view — it is a single-line description limited to 200 bytes. `StaticDetails` is shown in the Workflow details view, supports multi-line Markdown, and has a much larger limit of 20K bytes. Both are set at Workflow start time via `WorkflowOptions` and cannot be changed after the Workflow is started (unlike `Workflow.CurrentDetails`, which is dynamic but only appears in the details view under 'Current Details'). The other options invert limits or misattribute where fields appear in the UI.

questions.question: A .NET Worker is registered with a task queue called `"my-task-queue"` and has both `SayHelloWorkflow` and `MyActivities.SayHello` registered. The client starts the Workflow with the same task queue. During load testing, engineers observe that some Workflow Tasks complete but the Activity Task that invokes `SayHello` is never picked up, even though the Worker process is running. Which of the following is the most likely cause given how the .NET SDK worker registration works?

choices.text: The Worker was constructed with `.AddWorkflow<SayHelloWorkflow>()` but the Activity was registered as `activities.SayHello` (an instance method delegate) rather than the full `MyActivities` class type, and the Worker process crashed when the Activity Task arrived because the instance was garbage-collected.

Activity Tasks and Workflow Tasks are dispatched on the same task queue but the .NET SDK uses separate internal polling loops; if the Worker's Activity polling loop was never started because `AddActivity` was omitted, Activity Tasks queue up indefinitely while Workflow Tasks continue to be processed normally.

The Temporal server routes Activity Tasks to the task queue of the Workflow that scheduled them only if the Activity's registered task queue matches the Workflow's task queue exactly; a case-sensitive mismatch between `"my-task-queue"` at registration and `"My-Task-Queue"` in `ActivityOptions` would silently drop Activity Tasks.

The `StartToCloseTimeout` on the `ActivityOptions` was set to `TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5)`, which the server interprets as a scheduling deadline rather than an execution deadline; Activities not started within 5 minutes are dropped rather than retried.

questions.explanation: In the .NET SDK, Activities and Workflows are registered separately on the `TemporalWorkerOptions` via `AddActivity` and `AddWorkflow`. If `AddActivity` is omitted, the Worker will successfully poll for and execute Workflow Tasks but will never register a poller for Activity Tasks on that task queue. From the server's perspective, Activity Tasks simply accumulate on the queue with no worker to drain them. The other options describe real but distinct failure modes: garbage collection of an activity instance would cause a runtime exception rather than silent non-polling; a task queue name mismatch would route the task to a different queue rather than dropping it; and `StartToCloseTimeout` governs execution time, not scheduling — tasks not started are retried via `ScheduleToStartTimeout` (which defaults to unlimited) not silently dropped.

choices.text: `Workflow.CurrentDetails` mutations are durable commands written to the event history as `UpsertWorkflowSearchAttributes` events, so they survive replay identically. The stale UI state was caused by the Activity `Summary` exceeding 200 bytes, which silently truncated the field, corrupting the metadata store and rolling back `CurrentDetails` to its last valid snapshot.

`Workflow.CurrentDetails` is an in-memory, mutable property that is NOT recorded as a durable history event. On replay, the Worker re-executes workflow code deterministically to rebuild state, so `CurrentDetails` is set again as each line is replayed. The stale UI value seen after the crash was a transient visibility lag — the Worker had not yet replayed past the last `CurrentDetails` assignment when the UI was checked, and the value became current once replay completed.

Activity `Summary` values are written into the `ActivityTaskScheduled` history event and are therefore durable, but `Workflow.CurrentDetails` is stored in a separate side-channel outside the history. After a crash, Temporal rolls back the side-channel to the last server-side checkpoint, which corresponds to the most recent completed Activity, explaining the stale value until the Workflow re-emitted it.

`Workflow.CurrentDetails` and Activity `Summary` fields are both flushed to the server only when the workflow task completes. If the Worker crashes mid-task before the completed task is acknowledged, the server discards all pending metadata mutations for that task. The timer `Summary` is also lost and must be re-emitted, but because timer delays block replay, `CurrentDetails` remains permanently stale unless the engineer manually resets the workflow.

questions.explanation: The critical insight here is understanding which Temporal metadata is durable (in the event history) versus which is ephemeral (in-memory, re-derived during replay). `Workflow.CurrentDetails` is an in-memory property on the workflow execution context — it is NOT written as a dedicated history event. This means the server does not independently store each assignment; rather, the current value at any moment is whatever the workflow code last set it to during the current (or most recent completed) workflow task. Activity `Summary` fields are embedded in the `ActivityTaskScheduled` event and are therefore durable, and timer `Summary` fields go into the `TimerStarted` event similarly. But `CurrentDetails` is different: it is sent to the server as part of the workflow task completion payload, meaning it reflects the state at the end of the last successfully completed workflow task. When a Worker crashes mid-task and restarts, the server schedules a new workflow task from the last committed history point. During replay, the SDK re-executes the workflow code synchronously up to the replay frontier, re-setting `CurrentDetails` at each line. The UI observing the workflow during this replay window — before the Worker reaches the in-progress line — will see the value from the last completed task, which appears 'stale'. Once replay finishes and the Worker completes the current workflow task, `CurrentDetails` is updated on the server. This is not a bug: it is the expected interaction between replay semantics and mutable workflow metadata. The practical production lesson is: never assume `CurrentDetails` reflects real-time workflow position during replay or slow task execution — it is a lagging indicator bounded by workflow task completion boundaries. This also clarifies why `StaticSummary` / `StaticDetails` (set at workflow start) are always consistent: they are embedded in the `WorkflowExecutionStarted` event and never change.

choices.text: `StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are written into the `WorkflowExecutionStarted` history event and are therefore immutable and always present for any completed workflow. Activity `Summary` fields are embedded in `ActivityTaskScheduled` events at schedule time, so they are durable — their absence for old runs simply means those runs predated the code that set `Summary`. `CurrentDetails` reflects the last workflow-task-completion payload, so old runs that never re-assigned it show only the initial value.

`StaticSummary` is stored in the visibility store as a search attribute, while `StaticDetails` is stored separately in an archival backend. Both are queryable post-completion because they are indexed at workflow start. Activity `Summary` and `CurrentDetails` are written only to the in-memory worker cache, so they are lost when the workflow completes and the worker recycles its execution context, explaining their absence for historical runs.

All four metadata fields — `StaticSummary`, `StaticDetails`, `CurrentDetails`, and Activity `Summary` — are stored exclusively in the event history blob. When a workflow closes, Temporal compacts the history into a summary record containing only the start and close events, which preserves `StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` but truncates mid-execution metadata like `CurrentDetails` mutations and Activity `Summary` fields, explaining the missing data for completed runs.

`StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are lost once a workflow closes because the visibility store entry expires. Old workflows appear to show them only because the Temporal UI fetches them from a local browser cache populated when the workflow was first viewed while running. Activity `Summary` fields are durable in the history indefinitely, but the UI rendering layer filters them out for closed workflows to reduce payload size in the list view.

questions.explanation: Understanding where each metadata field is persisted — and when — is essential for reasoning about what the UI can show for historical workflows. `StaticSummary` and `StaticDetails` are set in `WorkflowOptions` at start time and are embedded in the `WorkflowExecutionStarted` event, the very first event in every workflow's history. Because that event is immutable and retained for the namespace's retention period (and indefinitely if archival is enabled), these values are always recoverable for any workflow whose history exists. Activity `Summary` fields are embedded in `ActivityTaskScheduled` events, which are written to history at the moment the activity is scheduled during a workflow task completion. This means: for workflows that ran before the code change, no `ActivityTaskScheduled` event in their history ever contained a `Summary` field, so none is shown — not a data loss issue, but a simple code-vintage issue. `Workflow.CurrentDetails` is sent as part of the workflow task completion response; each update is carried in the corresponding `WorkflowTaskCompleted` event. For old workflows that never executed the updated code, the only `CurrentDetails` value ever sent was from the initial workflow task, so that is all the UI can display. The production lesson: metadata enrichment is only as complete as the code that was running at the time. Retrofitting metadata requires either a workflow reset (to replay with new code) or accepting that historical runs have sparse metadata. This question also illustrates why `StaticSummary`/`StaticDetails` are deliberately immutable by design — their durability guarantee comes from being anchored to the start event, making them ideal for compliance and audit tagging.
